skill.name: iterate-pr
skill.description: Guides an agent through fixing pull request CI failures, collecting review feedback, committing changes, and pushing updates until checks pass.
skill.summary: Automates the pull request feedback, fix, push, and CI wait loop.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis over-reported high-risk patterns: no weak cryptography, Ruby backtick execution, malware behavior, or prompt injection was found in SKILL.md. The real risk is operational: the skill instructs an authenticated agent to run GitHub CLI and Git commands that can read PR data, inspect logs, commit files, and push changes.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitHub and Git Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs the agent to run GitHub CLI and Git commands that read PR state, inspect CI logs, stage all changes, commit, and push to the remote branch. This is legitimate for PR automation, but it can alter repository contents and publish commits if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly shown in Markdown shell blocks and include authenticated PR inspection plus git add, commit, and push. The intent is legitimate, but the repository mutation risk is clear.

medium_findings.title: Unquoted Branch Substitution in Shell Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples use command substitution for the current branch in shell commands. If copied directly, unusual branch names can cause incorrect arguments or failed commands, even though this is guidance text rather than a packaged script.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is present exactly at the cited lines. The impact depends on whether the agent executes the examples verbatim and on branch naming.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Source URL Is Metadata Only
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URL appears in the front matter as source attribution. It is not an executable network request and does not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is contained in a metadata field at the top of the Markdown file. No code on that line performs a request.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Hits Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic functions, malware logic, system discovery, or host reconnaissance. They are skill description text, PR comment guidance, commit message text, and check URL guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the exact lines shows ordinary Markdown instructions. No evidence of weak cryptographic algorithm use or system reconnaissance was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated GitHub and Git Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs the agent to run GitHub CLI and Git commands that read PR state, inspect CI logs, stage all changes, commit, and push to the remote branch. This is legitimate for PR automation, but it can alter repository contents and publish commits if used without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly shown in Markdown shell blocks and include authenticated PR inspection plus git add, commit, and push. The intent is legitimate, but the repository mutation risk is clear.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unquoted Branch Substitution in Shell Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples use command substitution for the current branch in shell commands. If copied directly, unusual branch names can cause incorrect arguments or failed commands, even though this is guidance text rather than a packaged script.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is present exactly at the cited lines. The impact depends on whether the agent executes the examples verbatim and on branch naming.

content.user_title: Iterate Pull Requests Until CI Passes
content.value_statement: Pull request feedback loops can waste time when CI and reviews fail repeatedly. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a focused fix, push, and verify cycle.
actual_capabilities: Identifies the current pull request with GitHub CLI.
Checks CI status before reviewing comments.
Collects review comments, inline comments, and bot feedback.
Reads failed GitHub Actions logs before changing code.
Commits and pushes targeted fixes to the current branch.
Repeats the loop until checks pass or escalation is needed.
limitations: Requires an authenticated GitHub CLI installation.
Works only in a repository with a pull request for the current branch.
Can push commits, so users should review changes before execution.
Does not resolve ambiguous product or reviewer decisions automatically.

use_cases.title: Fix failing CI on an active pull request
use_cases.description: Use the skill to inspect checks, read logs, make targeted fixes, and wait for new results.

use_cases.title: Address human and bot review feedback
use_cases.description: Collect PR comments from reviewers and automation, then verify each issue before changing code.

use_cases.title: Maintain green quality gates before merge
use_cases.description: Repeat the check, fix, commit, and push cycle until required checks pass or help is needed.

prompt_templates.title: Check the current PR
prompt_templates.prompt: Use iterate-pr to inspect the current branch pull request and summarize failing checks before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Fix CI failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use iterate-pr to read failed CI logs, identify the real cause, make minimal fixes, and show me the planned commit.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve review feedback
prompt_templates.prompt: Use iterate-pr to gather human and bot review comments, validate each issue, and address only confirmed problems.

prompt_templates.title: Run the full PR loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Use iterate-pr to continue the feedback, fix, commit, push, and wait loop until checks pass or escalation is required.

output_examples.input: Inspect my current PR and tell me why CI is failing.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the PR number, lists failed checks, summarizes the relevant log failure, and proposes the smallest fix.

output_examples.input: Address all valid review feedback on this branch.
output_examples.output: The assistant separates valid comments from stale or incorrect feedback, applies focused edits, and prepares a concise commit summary.

output_examples.input: Keep iterating until required checks pass.
output_examples.output: The assistant cycles through CI status, fixes, commits, and pushes, then stops when checks pass or repeated failure needs user input.

best_practices: Review the planned changes before allowing commits or pushes.
Keep GitHub CLI permissions scoped to the repositories that need automation.
Escalate after repeated identical failures instead of making broad speculative changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run it on a branch without confirming the target pull request.
Do not use it to bypass reviewer decisions or merge policy.
Do not let it commit unrelated formatting, generated files, or local secrets.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill depends on an authenticated gh command being available in the repository environment.

faq.question: Can it push commits?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow includes staging files, committing changes, and pushing the current branch.

faq.question: Does it read CI logs?
faq.answer: Yes. It instructs the assistant to read failed GitHub Actions logs before diagnosing failures.

faq.question: Will it fix every review comment automatically?
faq.answer: No. It tells the assistant to validate feedback and ask for help when a decision needs clarification.

faq.question: Is this safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: It can be used in private repositories, but access depends on the GitHub CLI token and user approval policies.

faq.question: What should happen after repeated failures?
faq.answer: The skill says to ask for help after the same failure persists after three attempts.
